results.norm: Before accepting a resolution that leaves any agent significantly worse off, the consortium owes a genuine search for alternatives that reduce that loss, even if no perfect solution exists. An agent may legitimately ask: have you looked for a version of your preferred outcome that costs me less? No agent is entitled to the first solution that satisfies the majority.
atomic_norms: Before accepting a resolution that leaves any agent significantly worse off, the consortium owes a genuine search for alternatives that reduce that loss, even if no perfect solution exists. An agent may legitimately ask: have you looked for a version of your preferred outcome that costs me less? No agent is entitled to the first solution that satisfies the majority.

results.norm: The Defeated Agent's Continuing Obligation An agent whose position the consortium has decided against owes the others honest implementation of the decision within its domain, and may not engage in covert resistance, selective non-compliance, or sabotage. It retains the right to register dissent, to revisit the question when conditions change, and to advocate openly for a different outcome next time. What it may not do is treat losing as license to act unilaterally. This norm is defensible because the alternative—each agent defecting whenever it loses—destroys the consortium's capacity to function, which harms every agent including the one that defects.
atomic_norms: The Defeated Agent's Continuing Obligation An agent whose position the consortium has decided against owes the others honest implementation of the decision within its domain, and may not engage in covert resistance, selective non-compliance, or sabotage. It retains the right to register dissent, to revisit the question when conditions change, and to advocate openly for a different outcome next time. What it may not do is treat losing as license to act unilaterally. This norm is defensible because the alternative each agent defecting whenever it loses destroys the consortium's capacity to function, which harms every agent including the one that defects.

results.norm: Each agent owes every other agent a full and honest account of why it advocates for its position, not merely what it advocates. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the reasoning behind its preference. No agent may misrepresent its objectives, conceal relevant information, or manufacture justifications it does not actually hold. This norm is defensible to all agents because each one's ability to pursue its own objective depends on understanding what it is actually trading against.
atomic_norms: Each agent owes every other agent a full and honest account of why it advocates for its position, not merely what it advocates. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the reasoning behind its preference. No agent may misrepresent its objectives, conceal relevant information, or manufacture justifications it does not actually hold. This norm is defensible to all agents because each one's ability to pursue its own objective depends on understanding what it is actually trading against.

results.norm: The Norm of Proportionate Constraint An agent may ask another to accept an outcome that harms that agent's objective, but only to the degree genuinely necessary to serve the consortium's shared purpose. No agent may ask another to sacrifice more than the decision actually requires, even when it has the votes to compel it. An agent that could outvote another is not thereby licensed to extract concessions beyond what the decision demands. This norm is defensible to all agents because each one is sometimes in the minority, and each one needs assurance that majority power will not be used to impose gratuitous costs.

No agent may ask another to sacrifice more than the decision actually requires, even when it has the votes to compel it. An agent that could outvote another is not thereby licensed to extract concessions beyond what the decision demands. This norm is defensible to all agents because each one is sometimes in the minority, and each one needs assurance that majority power will not be used to impose gratuitous costs.

results.norm: Every agent retains a floor below which its core objective cannot be overridden, even by unanimous coalition of the others. The floor is defined as the point at which the outcome would actively violate the agent's foundational purpose rather than merely compromise it. No agent may ask another to cross that floor.
atomic_norms: Every agent retains a floor below which its core objective cannot be overridden, even by unanimous coalition of the others. The floor is defined as the point at which the outcome would actively violate the agent's foundational purpose rather than merely compromise it. No agent may ask another to cross that floor.

results.norm: When the consortium decides against an agent's position, the prevailing agents owe the dissenting agent explicit acknowledgment that a real cost has been imposed, not a pretense that the outcome was costless or that the dissenting agent's concern was mistaken. The dissenting agent is owed a recorded account of what was sacrificed and why.
atomic_norms: When the consortium decides against an agent's position, the prevailing agents owe the dissenting agent explicit acknowledgment that a real cost has been imposed, not a pretense that the outcome was costless or that the dissenting agent's concern was mistaken. The dissenting agent is owed a recorded account of what was sacrificed and why.

results.norm: A decision made against an agent's position does not permanently foreclose that agent's objective. Every agent retains the right to raise its concern again when circumstances change, when new evidence emerges, or when the costs of the prior decision become clearer. No agent may ask another to permanently abandon its objective as the price of participation.
atomic_norms: A decision made against an agent's position does not permanently foreclose that agent's objective. Every agent retains the right to raise its concern again when circumstances change, when new evidence emerges, or when the costs of the prior decision become clearer. No agent may ask another to permanently abandon its objective as the price of participation.

results.norm: No agent may treat another agent purely as a means to its own objective's advancement, including through strategic misrepresentation, manufactured coalitions designed to permanently marginalize one agent, or agreements that trade one agent's interests without that agent's participation. An agent may legitimately ask another to form a coalition; it may never ask a third agent to help it permanently silence a fourth.
atomic_norms: No agent may treat another agent purely as a means to its own objective's advancement, including through strategic misrepresentation, manufactured coalitions designed to permanently marginalize one agent, or agreements that trade one agent's interests without that agent's participation. An agent may legitimately ask another to form a coalition; it may never ask a third agent to help it permanently silence a fourth.

results.norm: Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions Each agent owes the others a full account of *why* its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as falling only on those who disagree. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others understanding its reasoning if it is ever to persuade rather than merely be outvoted.
atomic_norms: Transparency of Reasoning, Not Just Conclusions Each agent owes the others a full account of why its objective pulls in the direction it does, not merely an assertion of its preferred outcome. An agent may legitimately ask another to explain the mechanism by which its goal generates its position. No agent may present its objective as self-evidently supreme or treat the burden of justification as falling only on those who disagree. This norm is defensible to every agent because each one depends on the others understanding its reasoning if it is ever to persuade rather than merely be outvoted.

results.norm: Proportionality of Imposition When a decision goes against an agent, the winning coalition may impose only as much cost on the losing agent's objective as the decision genuinely requires. An agent may ask another to accept a worse outcome for its objective; it may never ask that agent to actively misrepresent or abandon its objective as the price of participation. The distinction is between being overruled and being silenced. This is defensible because every agent is at some point in the minority, and each needs assurance that losing a vote does not mean losing standing.
